message:
feat(e2e): deterministic transform workload preset (bd-1w6k.4.2)

Add preset_deterministic_transform() to oplog: creates 3 namespaced
tables (_fsqlite_e2e_kv, _events, _blob) with indexes, populates with
seed-derived deterministic data, then performs update/delete transforms
with event logging. Designed for serial sha256 equality proof.

fsqlite executor now gracefully skips unsupported DDL (CREATE INDEX).
7 new tests added across oplog and both executors.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-1w6k.3.5","title":"Reports: JSONL run records + summary fields","description":"## Summary
Define and implement report output format.

## Requirements
- JSONL per run with:
  - engine metadata (sqlite version / fsqlite commit)
  - db_id + sha256 golden
  - workload + parameters
  - timings (total, per-phase)
  - verification outcomes (integrity, hashes)
  - errors (structured)

## Acceptance
- Every run produces a JSON object we can aggregate.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:21:21.860946202Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T07:31:13.555241418Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T07:31:13.555222493Z","close_reason":"Implemented RunRecordV1 JSONL schema and wired realdb-e2e run to emit JSONL (optional --output-jsonl / --pretty)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["e2e","harness","report"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.3.5","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.3","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:21:21.860946202Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.3.5","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.3.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:22:20.397351488Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1082,"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.3.5","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Acceptance Criteria

 {"id":"bd-1w6k.4","title":"Workloads: Deterministic Transforms + Massively Concurrent Writes","description":"Define and implement workload generators that can run against *unknown* real DB schemas safely.

Key idea:
- Prefer operating in our own namespace by creating dedicated tables like `_fsqlite_e2e_*` so workloads succeed on arbitrary DBs without depending on existing schema.

We will produce both:
- deterministic workloads (for sha256-identity checks)
- massively concurrent workloads (for MVCC speedup measurement)","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:17:50.080780447Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T04:17:50.080780447Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["e2e","perf","workload"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:17:50.080780447Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1w6k.4.1","title":"Workload framework: deterministic DSL + seeded generator","description":"## Summary
Define a minimal “workload DSL” (even if just structs) that can be executed by both engines.

## Requirements
- Deterministic seeded generation for statement sequences.
- Explicit phases: setup -> write phase -> verification queries.
- Ability to run in N workers with barrier start.

## Acceptance
- One workload implemented in the DSL runs identically on sqlite3 and fsqlite.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:21:22.092214193Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T04:59:17.049412020Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T04:59:17.049390650Z","close_reason":"Superseded by bd-1q00 (detailed workload generator with seeded RNG, code examples, operation mix)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["e2e","workload"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.4.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.3.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:22:20.610147384Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.4.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:21:22.092214193Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1083,"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.4.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Acceptance Criteria

-{"id":"bd-1w6k.4.2","title":"Workload: deterministic transform (sha256 proof)","description":"## Summary
Create the deterministic transformation workload used for sha256 equality checks.

## Design
Operate in our own namespace so it works on any DB:
- Create tables like `_fsqlite_e2e_kv`, `_fsqlite_e2e_events`, `_fsqlite_e2e_blob`
- Populate with deterministic data (fixed seed)
- Perform deterministic updates/deletes
- Create indexes to exercise B-tree maintenance

## Acceptance
- When run serially, both engines produce identical *canonical* hash outputs.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:21:22.329898169Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T07:35:32.673401468Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["compat","e2e","workload"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.4.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.3.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:22:21.040422447Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.4.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.3.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:22:21.257713Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.4.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.3.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:22:21.470939031Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.4.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:21:22.329898169Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.4.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.4.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:22:20.821084409Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.4.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.5.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:22:24.534450072Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.4.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.5.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:22:24.799539248Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.4.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.5.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:22:25.028740957Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1084,"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.4.2","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Acceptance Criteria
- Implementation matches the task scope and title
- All unit tests pass; code compiles without warnings under clippy pedantic+nursery
- Integration with existing harness framework verified
- No unsafe code, no panics in normal operation
","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:21:34Z"},{"id":1159,"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.4.2","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Cross-Reference (IvoryWaterfall audit)

**Relationship to bd-z0xm correctness beads**: This bead tests deterministic transforms with SHA-256 proof. The bd-z0xm tree has more granular beads that cover similar ground:
- bd-3aoy: Sequential INSERT flood (10K rows, SHA-256 comparison)
- bd-1ej1: Mixed DML workload (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE, 20K ops)
- bd-24uu: Transaction patterns (BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK/SAVEPOINT)

**Unique value of THIS bead**: Operates in its own namespace (`_fsqlite_e2e_kv` etc.) on existing databases rather than creating new tables. Also feeds bd-1w6k.4.4 (workload validation invariants) and bd-1w6k.8.2 (CI smoke test).

**These are complementary**: The bd-z0xm correctness beads test specific operation types in isolation; this bead tests a holistic deterministic transform pipeline. Both should be implemented.
","created_at":"2026-02-10T05:10:16Z"}]}

 {"id":"bd-1w6k.4.3","title":"Workload: massively concurrent writers (MVCC benchmark)","description":"## Summary
Create the massively concurrent write workload for MVCC speedup measurement.

## Design
- N worker threads/processes, each with its own connection.
- Each worker writes in short transactions with retry-on-busy.
- Two modes:
  1. disjoint partitions (should scale well under MVCC)
  2. controlled contention (measure conflict rate)

## Measurements
- throughput (txns/sec)
- p50/p95 latency
- abort/retry counts

## Acceptance
- Harness can run with `--concurrency 1,2,4,8,16` and report scaling.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:21:22.560126302Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T07:32:44.993176971Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["e2e","mvcc","perf","workload"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.4.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.2.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:22:22.538584907Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.4.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.3.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:22:22.111826149Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.4.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.3.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:22:22.326011297Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.4.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:21:22.560126302Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.4.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.4.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:22:21.902720184Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1085,"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.4.3","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Acceptance Criteria
- Implementation matches the task scope and title
- All unit tests pass; code compiles without warnings under clippy pedantic+nursery
- Integration with existing harness framework verified
- No unsafe code, no panics in normal operation
","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:21:34Z"},{"id":1161,"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.4.3","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Cross-Reference (IvoryWaterfall audit)

**Relationship to bd-z0xm beads**:
- bd-3rze: Concurrent write throughput (2/4/8/16 threads) — measures performance
- bd-244z: Concurrent multi-thread writes with logical equivalence — measures correctness

**Unique value of THIS bead**: Focuses specifically on MVCC scaling with two modes (disjoint partitions vs controlled contention) and measures abort/retry counts. The bd-z0xm beads measure throughput and correctness separately.

**These are complementary**: This bead adds MVCC-specific measurements (conflict rate, abort counts, scaling curve) that neither bd-3rze nor bd-244z explicitly cover. All three should be implemented.

**Dependency note**: This bead requires bd-1w6k.2.4 (public MVCC toggle), which is unique infrastructure that bd-z0xm beads also need but don't explicitly depend on. Implementers of bd-3rze and bd-244z should coordinate with this bead.
","created_at":"2026-02-10T05:10:20Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1w6k.4.4","title":"Workload validation: logical invariants + per-table checksums","description":"## Summary
Implement post-workload validation queries that ensure both engines produced the same logical results.

## Examples
- row counts per table partition
- aggregate checksums over deterministic columns
- invariants (no duplicates, foreign key constraints if used)

## Acceptance
- Validation fails with a crisp diff when outputs diverge.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:21:22.783149291Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T04:22:22.753452713Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["e2e","verify","workload"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.4.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:21:22.783149291Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.4.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.4.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:22:21.688511342Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.4.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.4.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:22:22.753376269Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1086,"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.4.4","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Acceptance Criteria
- Implementation matches the task scope and title
- All unit tests pass; code compiles without warnings under clippy pedantic+nursery
- Integration with existing harness framework verified
- No unsafe code, no panics in normal operation
","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:21:34Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1w6k.5","title":"Verification: Canonicalization + sha256 + Integrity Checks","description":"Implement validation and “byte identity” proof machinery.

Because raw file bytes can differ due to WAL state and layout choices, we will support 2 comparison tiers:
1. **Strict hash:** sha256 of the raw DB file (only expected to match when both engines are byte-for-byte identical).
2. **Canonical hash:** sha256 after a deterministic canonicalization step (e.g., checkpoint + VACUUM INTO) so equality reflects logical equivalence.

Also includes integrity checks and per-table checksums.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:17:50.281836304Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T04:17:50.281836304Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["compat","e2e","verify"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.5","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:17:50.281836304Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-1w6k.7","title":"Demos: Corruption Injection + Recovery (sqlite3 vs fsqlite)","description":"End-to-end corruption demos:
- corrupt copies of DB/WAL files in controlled ways
- show `sqlite3` failure modes (detected corruption, loss of last commit, unrecoverable pages)
- show FrankenSQLite recovery using WAL-FEC / RaptorQ repair symbols (when implemented)

This epic is intentionally narrative-driven: it should produce a compelling demo + logs.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:17:50.699143478Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T04:17:50.699143478Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["corruption","e2e","raptorq","recovery"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.7","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:17:50.699143478Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1w6k.7.1","title":"Tool: corruption injector (bitflips, truncation, page damage)","description":"## Summary
Implement a corruption injector that operates on **copies only**.

## Corruption types (v1)
- flip random bits in a WAL frame
- truncate WAL file mid-frame (torn write simulation)
- zero out a DB page region

## Safety
- Require explicit input path under `runs/` or `quarantine/`.
- Never accept paths under `golden/`.

## Acceptance
- Injector can produce a corrupted copy deterministically (seeded).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:21:24.796869476Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T06:53:30.313996908Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T06:53:30.313972372Z","close_reason":"Superseded by bd-2e3s (corruption injection framework already implemented in crates/fsqlite-e2e)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["corruption","e2e"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.7.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.1.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:22:26.542983200Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.7.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.3.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:22:26.324338491Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.7.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:21:24.796869476Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1094,"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.7.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Acceptance Criteria
- Implementation matches the task scope and title
- All unit tests pass; code compiles without warnings under clippy pedantic+nursery
- Integration with existing harness framework verified
- No unsafe code, no panics in normal operation
","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:21:36Z"},{"id":1158,"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.7.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Cross-Reference (IvoryWaterfall audit)

**This bead is superseded by bd-2e3s** (Build corruption injection framework), which is CLOSED and fully implemented in `crates/fsqlite-e2e/`.

The bd-2e3s implementation includes all corruption types listed here (bitflips, page zeroing, WAL frame corruption) plus additional patterns (header zeroing, partial page corruption, sidecar corruption). It also has stronger safety guarantees and returns structured `CorruptionReport` artifacts.

**Additional coverage**: bd-1odl adds 28 unit tests for the corruption injector.

**Recommendation**: Close this bead as superseded by bd-2e3s once its blockers are resolved.
","created_at":"2026-02-10T05:10:14Z"}]}
-{"id":"bd-1w6k.7.2","title":"Plan: corruption scenarios + expected outcomes","description":"## Summary
Design the specific demo scenarios we want to showcase.

## Target narratives
1. Corrupt WAL after a successful commit.
   - sqlite3: last commit becomes unreadable/ignored (data loss).
   - fsqlite: repair via WAL-FEC symbols (data recovered).
2. Bitrot in a committed DB page.
   - sqlite3: detects corruption; cannot self-heal.
   - fsqlite: repair via available symbols / redundancy.

## Acceptance
- For each scenario, define:
  - setup steps
  - corruption injection parameters
  - expected behavior for sqlite3 and fsqlite
  - success criteria","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:21:25.007683219Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T07:42:59.201150262Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T07:42:49.964841181Z","close_reason":"7 scenarios covering full corruption gradient: single-bit, multi-frame, beyond-tolerance, header destruction, page corruption, page zeroing, sidecar corruption. Each with setup steps, injection params, expected behavior for both engines, success criteria.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["corruption","e2e","recovery"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.7.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.2.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:22:27.282740725Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.7.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:21:25.007683219Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.7.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.7.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:22:26.809517904Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1095,"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.7.2","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Acceptance Criteria

","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:21:36Z"},{"id":1173,"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.7.2","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## bd-1w6k.7.2: Corruption Scenarios + Expected Outcomes — PLAN

### Overview

Seven scenarios covering the full corruption gradient from minimal single-bit errors through catastrophic header destruction. Each scenario is designed to showcase the WAL-FEC/DB-FEC recovery advantage that FrankenSQLite provides over C SQLite.

All scenarios use the deterministic `CorruptionInjector` from `fsqlite-e2e/src/corruption.rs` and the `RecoveryDemoConfig` toggle from bd-1w6k.2.5 to run each case twice (recovery OFF = emulate C SQLite, recovery ON = demonstrate repair).

---

### Scenario 1: Single-Bit WAL Frame Corruption (Minimal, Recoverable)

**Setup:**
1. Create a fresh database with `PRAGMA journal_mode=wal; PRAGMA page_size=4096;`
2. Insert 10 rows into table `accounts` inside a committed WAL transaction
3. Generate `.wal-fec` sidecar with R=4 repair symbols
4. Inject: `BitFlip { byte_offset: WAL_HEADER_SIZE + FRAME_SIZE + 24 + 100, bit_position: 3 }` (corrupts byte 100 of frame 1's page data)

**Expected — C SQLite (recovery OFF):**
- WAL checksum chain breaks at frame 1
- `PRAGMA integrity_check` reports corruption
- All frames after corruption point are lost (last committed data invisible)
- Query returns fewer rows than inserted

**Expected — FrankenSQLite (recovery ON):**
- xxh3_128 detects frame 1 page mismatch
- WAL-FEC recovery: K source symbols + R repair symbols available, R >= 1 corrupted frame
- `WalFecRecoveryOutcome::Recovered` returned
- All 10 rows intact; `PRAGMA integrity_check` clean

**Success Criteria:**
- Recovery log shows `outcome_is_recovered: true`, `validated_repair_symbols >= 1`
- C SQLite query result differs from pre-corruption snapshot
- FrankenSQLite query result matches pre-corruption snapshot exactly

---

### Scenario 2: WAL Multi-Frame Corruption Within Tolerance (R=4, 3 frames damaged)

**Setup:**
1. Database with 8+ committed WAL frames (multi-row transactions)
2. Generate `.wal-fec` sidecar with R=4
3. Inject: `WalFrameCorrupt { frame_numbers: [1, 3, 5], seed: 42 }`

**Expected — C SQLite:**
- Checksum chain breaks at frame 1; frames 2-7 also lost
- Data loss: all uncommitted-to-disk rows after frame 0

**Expected — FrankenSQLite:**
- 3 corrupted frames < R=4 tolerance
- Full recovery via RaptorQ decode
- All data intact

**Success Criteria:**
- DecodeProof artifact emitted with `k_source`, `r_repair`, and corrupted frame list
- Recovery log: `required_symbols <= available_symbols`

---

### Scenario 3: WAL Corruption Beyond Tolerance (R=4, 5 frames damaged)

**Setup:**
1. Same as Scenario 2 but with 8 WAL frames and R=4
2. Inject: `WalFrameCorrupt { frame_numbers: [0, 1, 2, 3, 4], seed: 99 }`

**Expected — C SQLite:**
- Total WAL data loss (all frames corrupted)

**Expected — FrankenSQLite:**
- 5 corrupted frames > R=4 tolerance
- `WalFecRecoveryOutcome::TruncateBeforeGroup` returned
- Fallback reason: `InsufficientSymbols`
- Graceful degradation: database still openable from last checkpoint, partial data loss same as C SQLite

**Success Criteria:**
- Recovery log: `fallback_reason: InsufficientSymbols`
- No crash, no panic — graceful fallback
- FrankenSQLite data matches C SQLite data (both truncated)

---

### Scenario 4: Database Header Destruction

**Setup:**
1. Fully committed database (WAL checkpointed) with multiple tables
2. Inject: `HeaderZero` (zeros first 100 bytes of page 1)

**Expected — C SQLite:**
- Magic bytes destroyed ("SQLite format 3\0" gone)
- Database cannot be opened at all
- `rusqlite::Connection::open()` returns error

**Expected — FrankenSQLite:**
- DB-FEC sidecar has page 1 in group G=1 with R=4 (400% redundancy)
- Header page reconstructed from repair symbols
- Database opens normally; all data accessible

**Success Criteria:**
- C SQLite open fails with clear error
- FrankenSQLite opens, `PRAGMA integrity_check` clean, all rows present
- Recovery log shows header page repair

---

### Scenario 5: Single Database Page Corruption (Mid-Table Data)

**Setup:**
1. Database with 100+ rows spanning multiple pages (page 5 contains data)
2. Generate DB-FEC sidecar
3. Inject: `PagePartialCorrupt { page_number: 5, offset_within_page: 64, length: 256, seed: 77 }`

**Expected — C SQLite:**
- `PRAGMA integrity_check` reports page 5 corruption
- Queries touching page 5 return garbled or error
- No self-repair capability

**Expected — FrankenSQLite:**
- xxh3_128 hash mismatch detected on page 5
- DB-FEC single-fault repair within group (XOR parity)
- Page 5 restored; all queries return correct results

**Success Criteria:**
- Page 5 data matches pre-corruption SHA-256 after repair
- C SQLite integrity_check fails; FrankenSQLite integrity_check passes

---

### Scenario 6: Complete Page Zeroing (Catastrophic Single-Page Loss)

**Setup:**
1. Database with B-tree index on column `id`
2. Inject: `PageZero { page_number: 3 }` (index root page)

**Expected — C SQLite:**
- Index completely destroyed; full table scan falls back but may error
- `PRAGMA integrity_check` reports critical corruption

**Expected — FrankenSQLite:**
- Full page recovery via DB-FEC group containing page 3
- Index intact after repair

**Success Criteria:**
- Index-based lookups work after FrankenSQLite recovery
- C SQLite requires `REINDEX` or fails queries

---

### Scenario 7: Sidecar Corruption (Degraded Recovery)

**Setup:**
1. Database with committed WAL + valid `.wal-fec` sidecar
2. Inject WAL corruption: `WalFrameCorrupt { frame_numbers: [2], seed: 55 }`
3. Also inject sidecar damage: `SidecarCorrupt { offset: 128, length: 64, seed: 66 }`

**Expected — FrankenSQLite (recovery ON):**
- Sidecar parse may fail or repair symbols may be invalid
- Fallback reason: `SidecarUnreadable` or `DecodeFailed`
- Graceful degradation to truncation (same as C SQLite behavior)
- No crash, no panic

**Success Criteria:**
- Recovery log: `fallback_reason` is one of the sidecar-related reasons
- System degrades gracefully to C SQLite equivalent behavior
- Demonstrates that FrankenSQLite is never WORSE than C SQLite

---

### Cross-Cutting Requirements

**For each scenario, the demo runner must:**
1. Create a fresh database copy from golden corpus (never corrupt originals)
2. Record pre-corruption SHA-256 of all pages
3. Apply corruption via `CorruptionInjector::inject()`
4. Run with `recovery_enabled: false` → capture C SQLite-equivalent results
5. Restore from golden copy, re-apply same corruption
6. Run with `recovery_enabled: true` → capture FrankenSQLite results
7. Compare results and emit structured report with:
   - `CorruptionReport` (affected pages, bytes, pattern)
   - `WalFecRecoveryLog` (outcome, symbols used, fallback reason)
   - Query result diff (pre vs post corruption, both engines)
   - DecodeProof artifact if recovery was attempted

**Determinism:**
- All seeds are fixed constants for reproducibility
- Page sizes are explicit (4096 default)
- WAL checkpoint state is controlled (pre/post checkpoint variants)

**Integration with E2E harness:**
- Use `HarnessSettings` (bd-1w6k.2.3) for consistent PRAGMA configuration
- Use `RecoveryDemoConfig` (bd-1w6k.2.5) for toggle control
- Reports feed into `RunRecordV1` for JSONL persistence (bd-1w6k.3.5)
","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:42:42Z"},{"id":1174,"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.7.2","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## bd-1w6k.7.2 — Implementation Complete (ScarletElk)

### Summary
Created `corruption_scenarios.rs` module with 8 fully-defined demo scenarios covering both target narratives.

### Scenario Catalog

**Narrative 1 — WAL corruption after commit (4 scenarios):**
1. `wal_corrupt_within_tolerance`: 2 frames corrupted, R=4 → fsqlite recovers, sqlite3 truncates
2. `wal_single_bit_flip`: Single bit flip in frame payload → same outcome, subtle detection
3. `wal_corrupt_beyond_tolerance`: ALL frames corrupted, R=2 → both engines lose data
4. `wal_corrupt_recovery_disabled`: Recoverable corruption but toggle OFF → both truncate

**Narrative 2 — Database page corruption (2 scenarios):**
5. `db_header_zeroed`: Header zeroed → sqlite3 can't open, fsqlite can't repair (no FEC for DB pages yet)
6. `db_page_bitrot`: Page 2 partial corrupt → sqlite3 detects but can't heal

**Edge cases (2 scenarios):**
7. `sidecar_damaged`: WAL-FEC sidecar itself corrupted → falls back to truncation
8. `wal_corrupt_no_sidecar`: No sidecar exists → graceful degradation like sqlite3

### Architecture
- `CorruptionScenario` struct with setup, injection, and expected-outcome fields
- `ScenarioCorruptionPattern` enum → converts to `CorruptionPattern` at runtime
- `ExpectedSqliteBehavior` / `ExpectedFsqliteBehavior` enums for assertions
- Filter functions: `wal_corruption_scenarios()`, `db_corruption_scenarios()`, `recoverable_scenarios()`, `unrecoverable_scenarios()`
- 11 unit tests all passing

### Quality Gates
- cargo check: clean
- clippy pedantic+nursery: clean
- cargo fmt: clean
- 11/11 tests passing

-{"id":"bd-1w6k.7.3","title":"Demo: sqlite3 behavior under corrupted WAL/DB","description":"## Summary
Implement the baseline sqlite3 side of the corruption demos.

## Requirements
- Attempt open + `PRAGMA integrity_check`.
- Attempt a small set of queries that should succeed if data intact.
- Capture error messages and evidence of lost data.

## Acceptance
- Demo runner produces a clear, reproducible transcript.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:21:25.229317487Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T04:22:28.174021148Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["corruption","e2e","sqlite"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.7.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.3.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:22:28.173979170Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.7.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:21:25.229317487Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.7.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.7.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:22:27.952313463Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1096,"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.7.3","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Acceptance Criteria

+{"id":"bd-1w6k.7.2","title":"Plan: corruption scenarios + expected outcomes","description":"## Summary
Design the specific demo scenarios we want to showcase.

## Target narratives
1. Corrupt WAL after a successful commit.
   - sqlite3: last commit becomes unreadable/ignored (data loss).
   - fsqlite: repair via WAL-FEC symbols (data recovered).
2. Bitrot in a committed DB page.
   - sqlite3: detects corruption; cannot self-heal.
   - fsqlite: repair via available symbols / redundancy.

## Acceptance
- For each scenario, define:
  - setup steps
  - corruption injection parameters
  - expected behavior for sqlite3 and fsqlite
  - success criteria","design":"## Scope
This bead is the **design/spec** for the corruption demos implemented in:
- `bd-1w6k.7.3` (sqlite3 baseline behavior)
- `bd-1w6k.7.4` (FrankenSQLite recovery path)

The output of this bead must be a deterministic, machine-checkable *scenario catalog* (setup + corruption injection + expected outcomes) so downstream demo code can be implemented with minimal additional judgment.

## Safety Invariants (Non-Negotiable)
- Never touch `/dp/...` originals.
- Treat `sample_sqlite_db_files/golden/` as immutable.
- All corruption must operate on **copies** created per-run (under a run scratch dir).
- Refuse to corrupt anything under `sample_sqlite_db_files/golden/`.
- Always record SHA-256 for: pristine working copy, corrupted copy, and any recovered outputs.

## WAL Lifecycle Gotcha
Empirically, on this environment SQLite may **remove/truncate the WAL file when the last connection closes**. Do not rely on `*.db-wal` still existing after closing the final handle.

Operational rule for demos/tests:
- Snapshot/copy the WAL bytes **before** the last connection closes, or
- Keep a reader connection open while copying, or
- Backup WAL bytes and restore them for replay.

## Scenario Catalog (v1)
This catalog is implemented in code as `crates/fsqlite-e2e/src/corruption_scenarios.rs` and mirrored here so future work does not depend on rediscovering intent.

### Narrative 1: WAL Corruption After Commit
These scenarios corrupt WAL frames after a successful commit.

1. `wal_corrupt_within_tolerance`
- Setup: insert 100 rows; enable WAL-FEC; provision `R=4` repair symbols.
- Inject: corrupt WAL frames `[0, 1]` with seed `42`.
- sqlite3 expected: `WalTailTruncated` (truncate at first bad checksum; lose committed data).
- fsqlite expected: `FullRecovery` (recovery enabled).

2. `wal_single_bit_flip`
- Setup: insert 100 rows; WAL-FEC on; `R=4`.
- Inject: flip a single bit in WAL frame 0 payload (`bit_position=3`).
- sqlite3 expected: `WalTailTruncated`.
- fsqlite expected: `FullRecovery` (detect + repair).

3. `wal_corrupt_beyond_tolerance`
- Setup: insert 100 rows; WAL-FEC on; `R=2`.
- Inject: corrupt **all** WAL frames; seed `99`.
- sqlite3 expected: `WalTailTruncated`.
- fsqlite expected: `RepairExceedsCapacity` (must fall back to truncation/data loss).

4. `wal_corrupt_recovery_disabled`
- Setup: insert 100 rows; WAL-FEC on; `R=4`.
- Inject: corrupt WAL frames `[0, 1]`; seed `42`.
- sqlite3 expected: `WalTailTruncated`.
- fsqlite expected: `RecoveryDisabled` (toggle demonstrates “behave like sqlite3”).

### Narrative 2: Database Bitrot (Committed DB File Damage)
These scenarios corrupt the `.db` file itself.

5. `db_header_zeroed`
- Setup: insert 50 rows; WAL-FEC off.
- Inject: zero out the 100-byte SQLite header.
- sqlite3 expected: `OpenFails`.
- fsqlite expected: `RepairExceedsCapacity` for now (future: header redundancy).

6. `db_page_bitrot`
- Setup: insert 50 rows; WAL-FEC off.
- Inject: corrupt 128 bytes of database page `2` (seed `77`).
- sqlite3 expected: `IntegrityCheckFails`.
- fsqlite expected: `RepairExceedsCapacity` for now (future: page checksums/repair).

### Edge Cases
7. `sidecar_damaged`
- Setup: insert 100 rows; WAL-FEC on; `R=4`.
- Inject: corrupt WAL-FEC sidecar region (offset `64`, length `512`, seed `55`).
- sqlite3 expected: `WalTailTruncated`.
- fsqlite expected: `SidecarDamaged` (fallback to truncation).

8. `wal_corrupt_no_sidecar`
- Setup: insert 100 rows; WAL-FEC off.
- Inject: corrupt WAL frames `[0, 1]`; seed `42`.
- sqlite3 expected: `WalTailTruncated`.
- fsqlite expected: degrade gracefully (no FEC available).

## Runner Implementation Notes (For bd-1w6k.7.3 / bd-1w6k.7.4)
- Setup should create/use a dedicated marker table (e.g., `fsqlite_e2e_marker`) so row-count/content assertions don’t depend on fixture schema.
- For each scenario and engine, record:
  - `PRAGMA integrity_check` outcome
  - marker row count + a deterministic checksum of marker contents
  - stdout/stderr snippets (sqlite3 CLI) and structured outcomes (fsqlite)
  - SHA-256 of the post-run canonical DB artifacts
- A scenario is “pass” if the observed outcome matches the expected behavior class (e.g., `WalTailTruncated` vs `FullRecovery`) and the machine-checkable assertions agree.

## Acceptance Mapping
This bead is complete when the above catalog exists (and stays deterministic) such that `bd-1w6k.7.3` and `bd-1w6k.7.4` can proceed without further design work.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:21:25.007683219Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T07:44:10.209732449Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T07:42:49.964841181Z","close_reason":"7 scenarios covering full corruption gradient: single-bit, multi-frame, beyond-tolerance, header destruction, page corruption, page zeroing, sidecar corruption. Each with setup steps, injection params, expected behavior for both engines, success criteria.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["corruption","e2e","recovery"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.7.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.2.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:22:27.282740725Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.7.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:21:25.007683219Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.7.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1w6k.7.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:22:26.809517904Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1095,"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.7.2","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Acceptance Criteria
- Implementation matches the task scope and title
- All unit tests pass; code compiles without warnings under clippy pedantic+nursery
- Integration with existing harness framework verified
- No unsafe code, no panics in normal operation
","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:21:36Z"},{"id":1173,"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.7.2","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## bd-1w6k.7.2: Corruption Scenarios + Expected Outcomes — PLAN

### Overview

Seven scenarios covering the full corruption gradient from minimal single-bit errors through catastrophic header destruction. Each scenario is designed to showcase the WAL-FEC/DB-FEC recovery advantage that FrankenSQLite provides over C SQLite.

All scenarios use the deterministic `CorruptionInjector` from `fsqlite-e2e/src/corruption.rs` and the `RecoveryDemoConfig` toggle from bd-1w6k.2.5 to run each case twice (recovery OFF = emulate C SQLite, recovery ON = demonstrate repair).

---

### Scenario 1: Single-Bit WAL Frame Corruption (Minimal, Recoverable)

**Setup:**
1. Create a fresh database with `PRAGMA journal_mode=wal; PRAGMA page_size=4096;`
2. Insert 10 rows into table `accounts` inside a committed WAL transaction
3. Generate `.wal-fec` sidecar with R=4 repair symbols
4. Inject: `BitFlip { byte_offset: WAL_HEADER_SIZE + FRAME_SIZE + 24 + 100, bit_position: 3 }` (corrupts byte 100 of frame 1's page data)

**Expected — C SQLite (recovery OFF):**
- WAL checksum chain breaks at frame 1
- `PRAGMA integrity_check` reports corruption
- All frames after corruption point are lost (last committed data invisible)
- Query returns fewer rows than inserted

**Expected — FrankenSQLite (recovery ON):**
- xxh3_128 detects frame 1 page mismatch
- WAL-FEC recovery: K source symbols + R repair symbols available, R >= 1 corrupted frame
- `WalFecRecoveryOutcome::Recovered` returned
- All 10 rows intact; `PRAGMA integrity_check` clean

**Success Criteria:**
- Recovery log shows `outcome_is_recovered: true`, `validated_repair_symbols >= 1`
- C SQLite query result differs from pre-corruption snapshot
- FrankenSQLite query result matches pre-corruption snapshot exactly

---

### Scenario 2: WAL Multi-Frame Corruption Within Tolerance (R=4, 3 frames damaged)

**Setup:**
1. Database with 8+ committed WAL frames (multi-row transactions)
2. Generate `.wal-fec` sidecar with R=4
3. Inject: `WalFrameCorrupt { frame_numbers: [1, 3, 5], seed: 42 }`

**Expected — C SQLite:**
- Checksum chain breaks at frame 1; frames 2-7 also lost
- Data loss: all uncommitted-to-disk rows after frame 0

**Expected — FrankenSQLite:**
- 3 corrupted frames < R=4 tolerance
- Full recovery via RaptorQ decode
- All data intact

**Success Criteria:**
- DecodeProof artifact emitted with `k_source`, `r_repair`, and corrupted frame list
- Recovery log: `required_symbols <= available_symbols`

---

### Scenario 3: WAL Corruption Beyond Tolerance (R=4, 5 frames damaged)

**Setup:**
1. Same as Scenario 2 but with 8 WAL frames and R=4
2. Inject: `WalFrameCorrupt { frame_numbers: [0, 1, 2, 3, 4], seed: 99 }`

**Expected — C SQLite:**
- Total WAL data loss (all frames corrupted)

**Expected — FrankenSQLite:**
- 5 corrupted frames > R=4 tolerance
- `WalFecRecoveryOutcome::TruncateBeforeGroup` returned
- Fallback reason: `InsufficientSymbols`
- Graceful degradation: database still openable from last checkpoint, partial data loss same as C SQLite

**Success Criteria:**
- Recovery log: `fallback_reason: InsufficientSymbols`
- No crash, no panic — graceful fallback
- FrankenSQLite data matches C SQLite data (both truncated)

---

### Scenario 4: Database Header Destruction

**Setup:**
1. Fully committed database (WAL checkpointed) with multiple tables
2. Inject: `HeaderZero` (zeros first 100 bytes of page 1)

**Expected — C SQLite:**
- Magic bytes destroyed ("SQLite format 3\0" gone)
- Database cannot be opened at all
- `rusqlite::Connection::open()` returns error

**Expected — FrankenSQLite:**
- DB-FEC sidecar has page 1 in group G=1 with R=4 (400% redundancy)
- Header page reconstructed from repair symbols
- Database opens normally; all data accessible

**Success Criteria:**
- C SQLite open fails with clear error
- FrankenSQLite opens, `PRAGMA integrity_check` clean, all rows present
- Recovery log shows header page repair

---

### Scenario 5: Single Database Page Corruption (Mid-Table Data)

**Setup:**
1. Database with 100+ rows spanning multiple pages (page 5 contains data)
2. Generate DB-FEC sidecar
3. Inject: `PagePartialCorrupt { page_number: 5, offset_within_page: 64, length: 256, seed: 77 }`

**Expected — C SQLite:**
- `PRAGMA integrity_check` reports page 5 corruption
- Queries touching page 5 return garbled or error
- No self-repair capability

**Expected — FrankenSQLite:**
- xxh3_128 hash mismatch detected on page 5
- DB-FEC single-fault repair within group (XOR parity)
- Page 5 restored; all queries return correct results

**Success Criteria:**
- Page 5 data matches pre-corruption SHA-256 after repair
- C SQLite integrity_check fails; FrankenSQLite integrity_check passes

---

### Scenario 6: Complete Page Zeroing (Catastrophic Single-Page Loss)

**Setup:**
1. Database with B-tree index on column `id`
2. Inject: `PageZero { page_number: 3 }` (index root page)

**Expected — C SQLite:**
- Index completely destroyed; full table scan falls back but may error
- `PRAGMA integrity_check` reports critical corruption

**Expected — FrankenSQLite:**
- Full page recovery via DB-FEC group containing page 3
- Index intact after repair

**Success Criteria:**
- Index-based lookups work after FrankenSQLite recovery
- C SQLite requires `REINDEX` or fails queries

---

### Scenario 7: Sidecar Corruption (Degraded Recovery)

**Setup:**
1. Database with committed WAL + valid `.wal-fec` sidecar
2. Inject WAL corruption: `WalFrameCorrupt { frame_numbers: [2], seed: 55 }`
3. Also inject sidecar damage: `SidecarCorrupt { offset: 128, length: 64, seed: 66 }`

**Expected — FrankenSQLite (recovery ON):**
- Sidecar parse may fail or repair symbols may be invalid
- Fallback reason: `SidecarUnreadable` or `DecodeFailed`
- Graceful degradation to truncation (same as C SQLite behavior)
- No crash, no panic

**Success Criteria:**
- Recovery log: `fallback_reason` is one of the sidecar-related reasons
- System degrades gracefully to C SQLite equivalent behavior
- Demonstrates that FrankenSQLite is never WORSE than C SQLite

---

### Cross-Cutting Requirements

**For each scenario, the demo runner must:**
1. Create a fresh database copy from golden corpus (never corrupt originals)
2. Record pre-corruption SHA-256 of all pages
3. Apply corruption via `CorruptionInjector::inject()`
4. Run with `recovery_enabled: false` → capture C SQLite-equivalent results
5. Restore from golden copy, re-apply same corruption
6. Run with `recovery_enabled: true` → capture FrankenSQLite results
7. Compare results and emit structured report with:
   - `CorruptionReport` (affected pages, bytes, pattern)
   - `WalFecRecoveryLog` (outcome, symbols used, fallback reason)
   - Query result diff (pre vs post corruption, both engines)
   - DecodeProof artifact if recovery was attempted

**Determinism:**
- All seeds are fixed constants for reproducibility
- Page sizes are explicit (4096 default)
- WAL checkpoint state is controlled (pre/post checkpoint variants)

**Integration with E2E harness:**
- Use `HarnessSettings` (bd-1w6k.2.3) for consistent PRAGMA configuration
- Use `RecoveryDemoConfig` (bd-1w6k.2.5) for toggle control
- Reports feed into `RunRecordV1` for JSONL persistence (bd-1w6k.3.5)
","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:42:42Z"},{"id":1174,"issue_id":"bd-1w6k.7.2","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## bd-1w6k.7.2 — Implementation Complete (ScarletElk)

### Summary
Created `corruption_scenarios.rs` module with 8 fully-defined demo scenarios covering both target narratives.

### Scenario Catalog

**Narrative 1 — WAL corruption after commit (4 scenarios):**
1. `wal_corrupt_within_tolerance`: 2 frames corrupted, R=4 → fsqlite recovers, sqlite3 truncates
2. `wal_single_bit_flip`: Single bit flip in frame payload → same outcome, subtle detection
3. `wal_corrupt_beyond_tolerance`: ALL frames corrupted, R=2 → both engines lose data
4. `wal_corrupt_recovery_disabled`: Recoverable corruption but toggle OFF → both truncate

**Narrative 2 — Database page corruption (2 scenarios):**
5. `db_header_zeroed`: Header zeroed → sqlite3 can't open, fsqlite can't repair (no FEC for DB pages yet)
6. `db_page_bitrot`: Page 2 partial corrupt → sqlite3 detects but can't heal

**Edge cases (2 scenarios):**
7. `sidecar_damaged`: WAL-FEC sidecar itself corrupted → falls back to truncation
8. `wal_corrupt_no_sidecar`: No sidecar exists → graceful degradation like sqlite3

### Architecture
- `CorruptionScenario` struct with setup, injection, and expected-outcome fields
- `ScenarioCorruptionPattern` enum → converts to `CorruptionPattern` at runtime
- `ExpectedSqliteBehavior` / `ExpectedFsqliteBehavior` enums for assertions
- Filter functions: `wal_corruption_scenarios()`, `db_corruption_scenarios()`, `recoverable_scenarios()`, `unrecoverable_scenarios()`
- 11 unit tests all passing

### Quality Gates
- cargo check: clean
- clippy pedantic+nursery: clean
- cargo fmt: clean
- 11/11 tests passing
